name: Kufri
tagline: Shimla's snow-play excuse in winter, crowded mini-zoo the rest of the year
why_visit: The only reliably snow-covered point near Shimla from late December to February. Skiing lessons run at the Nudla slope in season. Off-season it's a weekend-picnic crowd with a Himalayan Nature Park. Views of the Pir Panjal range on clear days.
kids_note: Winter snow is kid-magic for 5+. Horse-ride operators pressure; pay once at the entry, not along the way.

name: Chail
tagline: The quieter hill-alternative the Maharaja of Patiala built to stay away from Shimla
why_visit: Forty-five km from Shimla, Chail has the world's highest cricket ground (at 2,444m), a heritage Chail Palace (now HPTDC hotel), and pine forests that feel actually wild. Locals come here to escape Shimla crowds. Good 2-day trip if Shimla feels too packed.
highlights: Highest cricket ground in the world
kids_note: Very family-friendly. Palace garden is a safe roam for kids.
time_needed: 1-2 days if staying, full day if day-trip

name: Naldehra
tagline: Lord Curzon's favorite hilltop — now a 9-hole golf course surrounded by deodar
why_visit: Designed by Lord Curzon in 1905, this hilltop has India's oldest 9-hole golf course. Non-golfers come for the walks through deodar groves and the views across to the Dhauladhar range. Much less touristy than Kufri.
kids_note: Safe, quiet, stroller-unfriendly trails but plenty of open space.

name: Fagu
tagline: Apple orchards, terraced fields, and the cleanest panorama close to Shimla
why_visit: Twenty-two km on the Hindustan-Tibet highway toward Kufri. Apple orchards (August-October), terraced farms, pine forests. Less built up than Kufri. HPTDC runs a hotel here with good valley views.
kids_note: Orchard walks suit 4+. Winter cold is sharp — layer up.

name: Mashobra
tagline: Where the President's summer retreat sits, far quieter than Shimla
why_visit: Thirteen km from Shimla but feels like a different state. Apple orchards, the Presidential Retreat (partial public access), Wildflower Hall Oberoi, and quiet pine trails. Better-heeled weekenders pick this over the Mall Road circus.
highlights: Wildflower Hall (tea/meal even if not staying)
kids_note: Quiet, safe, but steep terrain — not stroller-friendly.

name: Tattapani
tagline: Hot sulphur springs on the Sutlej banks, 51km north of Shimla
why_visit: Named 'tatta pani' (hot water). Natural sulphur springs along the Sutlej river, believed to help joint pain. Camping and river rafting in season (March-June). Worth a day-trip if the hill-station air feels repetitive.
highlights: Natural hot springs (open to public, small fee)
kids_note: Hot springs are kid-safe at the public pool; keep eye on the river current.

name: Summer Hill
tagline: Quiet university-town end of Shimla with colonial-era bungalows
why_visit: Home to Himachal Pradesh University and the Indian Institute of Advanced Study (housed in Viceregal Lodge, 1888). Georgie Hill houses + pine walks. Much calmer than Mall Road.
highlights: Indian Institute of Advanced Study (Viceregal Lodge, tours)
kids_note: Peaceful. Viceregal Lodge tour sits well with 8+.

name: Tiger Hill
tagline: The Kanchenjunga sunrise viewpoint — leave Darjeeling at 4 AM or skip
why_visit: Highest point in the Darjeeling hills. On clear days you see Kanchenjunga (3rd highest in the world), Everest, and the full eastern Himalaya. Arrive by 5 AM — crowd is heavy by sunrise. October-December and April are the clearest windows.
kids_note: 4 AM start is rough for kids under 7. Dress warmly — 5°C common.

name: Mirik
tagline: Lake-town half-way to the Nepal border, less touristy than Darjeeling
why_visit: Forty-nine km from Darjeeling, centered on Sumendu Lake. Tea gardens on the drive (Okayti, Thurbo, Phuguri). Boating, pony-rides, and a cable-bridge walk across the lake. Good day-trip or quieter overnight.
highlights: Savour the Thurbo Tea Estate visit
kids_note: Easy trails, boating is supervised. Great family day-trip.

name: Batasia Loop
tagline: The toy-train spiral where the Darjeeling Himalayan Railway does a full loop
why_visit: A railway engineering trick — the train descends 360° through a garden, giving passengers a full Kanchenjunga view on clear days. The garden has a Gorkha War Memorial. Usually combined with the Tiger Hill pre-dawn run.
highlights: Toy train arrival (check schedule at Ghoom station)
Brief stop on the Darjeeling sunrise tour
kids_note: Kids love seeing the train loop. Garden is stroller-friendly.

name: Happy Valley Tea Estate
tagline: The oldest tea estate in Darjeeling (1854), 3km walk from Chowrasta
why_visit: Operational since 1854. Factory tours (when plucking season is on, March-November) show withering, rolling, oxidation, firing. Tea tasting is part of the tour. One of the few estates doing public tours inside Darjeeling town limits.
kids_note: 8+ kids find the factory interesting. Walking distance steep — stroller-unfriendly.

name: Senchal Lake
tagline: Darjeeling's quiet reservoir-lake, often combined with Tiger Hill on return
why_visit: Ten km from town. Built in 1910 as Darjeeling's water supply. Surrounded by Senchal Wildlife Sanctuary (barking deer, Himalayan black bear, occasional red panda). Good spot to decompress after the Tiger Hill chaos.
kids_note: Safe lakeside walks. Sanctuary is not stroller-friendly.

name: Padmaja Naidu Himalayan Zoological Park
tagline: India's highest zoo, focused on Himalayan species — red pandas and snow leopards
why_visit: The only Indian zoo breeding red pandas in captivity successfully. Also snow leopards, Himalayan wolves, Tibetan wolves, Siberian tigers. Combined with HMI (Himalayan Mountaineering Institute museum) since both are on the same campus. Morning visit best for animal activity.
kids_note: Kids 3+ love this. Zoo layout is manageable, some uphill.

name: Auroville
tagline: The Mother's 1968 experimental township — spiritual, divisive, globally unique
why_visit: Founded by Mirra Alfassa (the Mother) in 1968 as an experiment in human unity. The Matrimandir (inner chamber, golden orb) is the spiritual center — entry requires a prior visit to the visitor center and booking. Around 3,000 residents from 50+ countries. Cafes, bakeries, pottery studios, and organic farms are open to visitors.
kids_note: Matrimandir meditation is not for kids under 10. Cafes and farms are fine.

name: Paradise Beach
tagline: Sandbar beach reached only by a 30-min boat from Chunnambar backwaters
why_visit: Not walkable — the boat from Chunnambar Boathouse is the only access (fee includes return). Narrow strip of golden sand between backwater and sea. No shops, no restaurants — bring water and snacks. Busy Dec-Feb, quieter weekdays.
highlights: Chunnambar boat ride (20 min each way)
kids_note: Boat ride is safe (life jackets). Beach has no lifeguard — keep kids knee-deep.

name: Sri Aurobindo Ashram
tagline: The main ashram in White Town — Sri Aurobindo and the Mother's samadhi
why_visit: Founded 1926. The samadhi (resting place) of Sri Aurobindo and the Mother sits in the inner courtyard, covered in flowers. Quiet, short visit — silent meditation only. Separate bookstore stocks the complete works in 10+ languages.
kids_note: Silent — kids under 8 may find it boring. Keep visit short.

name: Promenade Beach (Rock Beach)
tagline: Central 1.5km seaside walk — the heart of Pondicherry after 6 PM
why_visit: Not a swimming beach (rocky seawall + rough surf). It's a walking promenade. Closed to traffic from 6 PM — that's when it comes alive with street food, joggers, French expats, and tourists. Gandhi statue, old lighthouse, and War Memorial mark the stretch.
kids_note: Safe promenade walk. Rocky shore means no swimming.

name: Serenity Beach
tagline: The surf beach 7km north — India's oldest surf school runs from here
why_visit: Unlike Promenade, this is a real beach — sandy, surfable. Kallialay Surf School (1-on-1 lessons, board rentals) operates here; small but well-run. Cafes on the road above. Quieter than Chennai beaches, busier than you'd hope on weekends.
kids_note: Minimum age for surf lessons is 6. Swimming fine for 10+. Watch currents.

name: Bharathi Park
tagline: White Town's central park — the French Quarter's open-air living room
why_visit: Named after Tamil poet Subramania Bharathi. Surrounded by the French Consulate, Puducherry Museum, and Raj Nivas (governor's residence). Morning yoga, evening couples, midday pensioners. The Aayi Mandapam monument in the center is a Pondy icon.
kids_note: Open grass, safe, stroller-friendly.

name: Mattupetty Dam
tagline: The dam-cum-reservoir 13km from Munnar — boating, tea, and shola forest
why_visit: Hydroelectric dam built in the 1940s. KTDC runs speed-boat and slow-boat rides on the reservoir (20-30 min). The Indo-Swiss Dairy Project (livestock research) is nearby. Views across the Kundala range. Usually combined with Echo Point and Top Station.
kids_note: Boating safe (life jackets). Wildlife on drive — don't leave car.

name: Eravikulam National Park
tagline: Home to the Nilgiri Tahr — India's only tur species — and peak of Anamudi
why_visit: Thousands of Nilgiri Tahr (endangered wild goat) live here, often visible grazing close to the trail. Rajamala section (the only tourist-accessible part) opens 7:30 AM. Anamudi, the highest peak south of the Himalayas (2,695m), is inside — closed to climbers. Park closes for breeding Feb-March.
kids_note: Short trail suits 5+. Morning entry recommended (crowds after 10 AM).

name: Top Station
tagline: The highest point on the old Kundala Valley railway, view into Tamil Nadu
why_visit: Originally the highest station of the now-defunct monorail that carried tea from Munnar to Kottagudi. Today just a viewpoint with tea stalls. On clear days, you see deep into Theni district, Tamil Nadu. The Neelakurinji flowers bloom here too (next cycle 2030).
kids_note: Viewpoint walk is short. Cliff edges — hold hands.

name: Echo Point
tagline: Shout-across-the-valley spot with a small lake — on the Mattupetty-Top Station route
why_visit: Natural echo caused by the hill contours. Small lake below, boat rides available. Tourist-coaches stop here, so it can feel crowded — visit early or on weekdays. Cardamom plantation viewpoint en route.
kids_note: Shouting into the valley = a kid's dream. Boat rides supervised.

name: Attukal Waterfalls
tagline: Rock-strewn waterfall on the Munnar-Pallivasal road, 9km from town
why_visit: Cascading falls over granite boulders along the Munnar-Pallivasal road. Small viewing area roadside — short walk down to the rocks. Peaks after June rains, reduces to trickle March-May.
highlights: Short photo stop en route to Munnar
kids_note: Slippery rocks, sharp drops — not safe for kids under 8 without close supervision.

name: Neelkanth Mahadev Temple
tagline: Shiva temple in the hills where legend says Neelkanth drank the ocean's poison
why_visit: Thirty-two km up from Rishikesh through serpentine forest road. Major Shiva pilgrimage site. Saran cave, Pandava cave nearby. Best in winter — summer heat + monsoon slides make access iffy. Weekend crowds heavy; weekdays quiet.
kids_note: Long twisty drive — motion sickness possible. Steps to temple are steep.

name: Vasistha Gufa
tagline: Meditation cave where Sage Vasistha is said to have sat — on the Ganga's banks
why_visit: Twenty-two km upstream on the Ganga, below Swargashram. Natural cave housing a Shiva Linga; Swami Purushottamananda's 20th-century ashram is above. Open 6 AM-7 PM. Quiet meditation space — no chatter, no photography inside.
kids_note: Silent space not suited to restless kids under 10. Trail to cave is short but rocky.

name: Beatles Ashram (Chaurasi Kutia)
tagline: Maharishi Mahesh Yogi's abandoned ashram where the Beatles wrote the White Album
why_visit: The Beatles stayed here in February-April 1968. Most of the White Album was written on these grounds. Abandoned in 1997, reopened to public in 2015 by the Rajaji National Park. Domed meditation kutirs are covered in Beatles-lyric street art, layered with newer murals from visiting artists. Entry ₹150 Indian / ₹600 foreign.
kids_note: Grounds are large but uneven. Kids 6+ find the ruins interesting.

name: Kunjapuri Devi Temple
tagline: Dawn-viewpoint temple with a 360° view of the Himalayan peaks
why_visit: One of the Siddha Peetha Shakti shrines. The real draw is the view — on clear winter mornings you see Swargarohini, Gangotri, Banderpunch. Sunrise is the money shot. Local operators run dawn tours from Rishikesh (₹800-1200 incl. jeep + trek).
highlights: Dawn sunrise (3-4 AM start from Rishikesh)
kids_note: Pre-dawn start tough for under-10. Short climb at the top is manageable.

name: Triveni Ghat
tagline: Rishikesh's main Ganga ghat — evening Aarti at 6 PM summer / 5 PM winter
why_visit: The confluence point in town — the most active Ganga ghat. Morning bathing crowds, evening aarti draws 500+ people. Crowded but the ritual experience is specific to Rishikesh (different from Varanasi's format). Arrive 45 min early for a bench view.
kids_note: Crowded during aarti — hold hands. Steps to water can be slippery.

name: Wagah Border (Attari Border)
tagline: India-Pakistan beating-retreat ceremony — 45 min of flag-dropping theatre daily
why_visit: Daily ceremony at sunset (timing varies — 4:15 PM winter, 5:15 PM summer). The two armies choreograph a goose-step showdown through the gate. Crowd chants, music, flag-lowering. Arrive 90 min early for a seat. 28 km from Golden Temple.
kids_note: Kids love the chanting + march. 90-min wait is rough — bring water, snacks not allowed inside.

name: Jallianwala Bagh
tagline: Site of the 1919 massacre — a somber 7-min walk from the Golden Temple
why_visit: On April 13, 1919, General Dyer ordered troops to fire on an unarmed crowd — 379+ killed, hundreds wounded. The walled garden preserves the bullet-marked wall, the well (where people jumped to escape), and the Martyrs' Memorial. Free entry.
kids_note: Somber tone; suitable for 8+ who can engage with the history.

name: Gobindgarh Fort
tagline: 300-year-old Sikh Empire fort, reopened as public museum-complex in 2017
why_visit: Built by Gujjar Singh Bhangi in 1760, later a key fort under Maharaja Ranjit Singh. Housed the Kohinoor briefly. Converted to public museum with sound-and-light show (7:30 PM). Café, handicraft stalls, and the 'Whispering Walls' exhibit on fort history.
kids_note: Sound-and-light is ok for 5+. Food court makes it easy for families.

name: Durgiana Temple (Silver Temple)
tagline: Hindu counterpart to the Golden Temple — marble + silver doors, 10 min walk away
why_visit: Built in 1921, inspired by the Golden Temple's architecture but Hindu (Durga and Vishnu). Marble + silver-plated doors. Much quieter than the Golden Temple but the sarovar (pool) + boli ritual mirrors the same tradition. Evening arti is intimate.
kids_note: Quiet, less crowded than Golden Temple — easier with kids.

name: Partition Museum
tagline: World's first museum dedicated to the 1947 Partition — opened 2017 in Town Hall
why_visit: First-of-its-kind museum on the largest forced migration in human history. Oral histories from survivors, personal artifacts (suitcases, letters), railway-carriage reconstruction. Emotional and carefully researched. Pay-what-you-can for Indians (minimum ₹10), ₹250 for foreigners.
kids_note: Heavy content. Best for 12+ kids. Takes 90 min minimum.

name: Chamundi Hill
tagline: Chamundeshwari Temple + the giant Nandi — 1000 steps or short drive up
why_visit: Named for Chamundeshwari (form of Durga). The temple dates to the 12th-c Hoysala era, expanded by Mysore Wodeyars. 1,008 steps from the base (pilgrims do it barefoot) or a 13-km drive. The 4.8m granite Nandi sculpture (16th-c) sits at step #800. Dussehra viewing from the top is iconic.
kids_note: Drive up is easy. 1000-step climb is not — pick based on kid age.

name: Brindavan Gardens
tagline: Illuminated musical fountain garden below the KRS dam — 21km from Mysore
why_visit: Laid out in 1932. Terraced Mughal-style gardens below the Krishna Raja Sagara Dam on the Kaveri. Musical fountain show at 7 PM (weekdays) / 7:30 PM (weekends) is the main draw — 30 min, synchronized water + lights + Bollywood music. Boating on the lake. Busy weekends.
kids_note: Fountain show is peak kid-magic. Boating supervised. Crowded weekends.

name: Somnathpur
tagline: The 13th-century Keshava Temple — Hoysala stonework at its most intact
why_visit: Built in 1268 by general Soma Dandanayaka. The only Hoysala temple with all three Vishnu forms (Janardhana, Keshava, Venugopala) intact. UNESCO-nominated. Intricate stellate floor plan, every inch carved. Much less visited than Belur/Halebid but arguably better-preserved.
kids_note: Older kids (10+) appreciate the carvings. Long drive + heat — early start.

name: St. Philomena's Cathedral
tagline: Neo-Gothic cathedral with 175-ft twin spires, built 1936
why_visit: Modeled on Cologne Cathedral. Twin spires dominate Mysore's skyline north of the palace. Stained-glass panels depict Christ's life. Catacombs below hold a relic of St. Philomena. Not huge like its European twins, but striking for the city. 2 km from the palace.
kids_note: Quiet, no entry fee. Catacombs dark — may be scary for under 6.

name: Mysore Rail Museum
tagline: Second-oldest rail museum in India — steam locomotives + Maharani's saloon
why_visit: Opened 1979. The Maharani's Saloon Carriage (early 1900s, used by Mysore royals) is the standout. Also: Chamundi locomotive, royal Austin-Durbar horse carriage on rails. Working miniature train ride for kids. Small, well-kept.
kids_note: Very kid-friendly. Train ride is a hit for 3-10 age group.

name: Sarnath
tagline: Where the Buddha gave his first sermon after enlightenment — Dhamek Stupa still stands
why_visit: One of the four key pilgrimage sites of Buddhism (along with Lumbini, Bodh Gaya, Kushinagar). 43m Dhamek Stupa (5th-c restoration of earlier Ashokan stupa), Ashoka Pillar base with the Lion Capital (now India's national emblem — actual capital in the museum). Sarnath Museum has exceptional Mauryan and Gupta sculpture. 10 km from Varanasi ghats, totally different vibe.
highlights: Mulagandha Kuti Vihar (modern monastery with murals)
kids_note: Open space, calm compared to Varanasi. Museum engages 8+ kids.

name: Ramnagar Fort
tagline: The Maharaja of Benares's 18th-century sandstone fort across the Ganga
why_visit: Built 1750 by Raja Balwant Singh. Still home to the current Maharaja (partial residence). Saraswati Bhawan museum has vintage cars, royal palanquins, astronomical clock, American cars, and armoury. Dilapidated in parts but atmospheric. Reach via boat from Assi Ghat or bridge (14km drive).
kids_note: Older kids (10+) engage. Fort has uneven steps + crumbling sections.

name: Manikarnika Ghat
tagline: The principal cremation ghat — where the fire is said to have burned since creation
why_visit: Not a 'visit' in the tourist sense — this is the main Hindu cremation ground. Pyres burn 24 hours. Photography is insensitive + forbidden in most parts. Observe from a respectful distance. Shiva's earring (mani-karnika) legend. Avoid 'guided tours' that promise rooftop views for payment — usually scams.
highlights: From boat is most respectful view
kids_note: Not suitable for kids under 12 — intense imagery.

name: Dashashwamedh Ghat
tagline: Varanasi's main ghat — the Ganga Aarti at 6:30 PM is the headline event
why_visit: The most active ghat in Varanasi. Boatmen, priests, flower-sellers, pilgrims. The evening Ganga Aarti (6:30 PM winter, 7 PM summer) is choreographed by 7 priests with fire, bells, incense, conch. Best viewed from a boat on the Ganga (₹150-300 per person shared). Crowded — arrive 45 min early for a land spot.
kids_note: Crowded — hold hands. Boat rides safe with life jackets (ask explicitly).

name: Assi Ghat
tagline: The southernmost major ghat — calmer, cleaner, where many yoga morning sessions happen
why_visit: At the confluence of the Assi stream with the Ganga. Less intense than Dashashwamedh. Subah-e-Banaras program runs 5 AM daily — Vedic chanting, yoga, morning aarti. Food carts, cafes, student hangouts at the top of the steps (Banaras Hindu University is 2 km away).
kids_note: Calmer than central ghats. Good introductory ghat for kids.

name: Naggar
tagline: The old Kullu capital — Nicholas Roerich's home + a 500-year-old castle
why_visit: Capital of Kullu for 1400 years until 1460. The Naggar Castle (now heritage HPTDC hotel) is built in the kath-kuni wood-stone style, 500+ years old. Nicholas Roerich (Russian painter, philosopher) lived here 1928-1947 — his home is now an art museum. Much quieter than Manali, 21km south via the left-bank road.
highlights: Left-bank road less touristy than NH-21
kids_note: Heritage sites engage 8+. Castle lunch terrace is stroller-friendly.

name: Hadimba Devi Temple
tagline: 1553 pagoda temple to Hadimba — demoness-turned-goddess of Mahabharata fame
why_visit: Built in 1553 by Raja Bahadur Singh. The 4-tiered wooden pagoda architecture is distinctive — Burmese monastic style meets Kullu kath-kuni. Set in a deodar grove — the whole setting is the point. Hadimba was the demoness Bhima married in the Mahabharata. Rock sanctum inside the temple (no idol; just a carved stone).
kids_note: Short walk from Mall Road. Yak/costume touts aggressive — firm 'no'.

name: Eklingji Temple
tagline: Mewar's royal family temple — Shiva as sovereign ruler, 22km north of Udaipur
why_visit: Built 734 CE by Bappa Rawal (founder of Mewar dynasty). Mewar rulers considered themselves Eklingji's diwans (prime ministers, not kings). Complex of 108 temples, main one in white marble + black stone Shiva. Maharana visits every Monday evening (historical tradition). 22km on the Nathdwara road.
kids_note: Quiet, family-friendly. Dress code: covered shoulders + knees.

name: Bagore Ki Haveli
tagline: 18th-century haveli on Gangaur Ghat — Rajasthani folk dance every evening
why_visit: Built in 1751 by Amar Chand Badwa, prime minister of Mewar. 100+ rooms, now a museum of courtly life — turbans, weapons, glass mosaics, the 'world's largest turban'. The real draw is the Dharohar folk dance show — 1 hour, 7 PM daily, traditional Kalbeliya + Ghoomar + fire-walking (₹90 Indian / ₹150 foreign). Rooftop puppet show follows.
kids_note: Folk dance is 60-min kid magic. Haveli has many steps — not stroller-friendly.

name: Fatehpur Sikri
tagline: Akbar's red-sandstone capital built 1571, abandoned 1585 — now a UNESCO site
why_visit: Akbar built his capital here in 1571 after a prophecy by Salim Chishti. Abandoned 14 years later (water shortages). Preserved almost intact. Buland Darwaza (world's highest gateway at 54m), Panch Mahal, Jodha Bai's Palace, Salim Chishti's Tomb. 40km from Taj Mahal — usually day-trip combo. UNESCO WHS.
highlights: Salim Chishti's Tomb (white marble inside red sandstone)
kids_note: Long walks, little shade — summer brutal. Oct-Feb ideal. 8+ kids engage with the scale.

name: Akbar's Tomb (Sikandra)
tagline: Akbar's mausoleum — less-visited, 10km from the Taj, deer + langur roam the grounds
why_visit: Built 1605-1613. Red sandstone with white marble inlay. Four-tiered design. Grounds are vast — deer, langurs, peacocks roam. The tomb itself is underground (small stairs down). Usually empty compared to the Taj. 10km northwest of Agra, often skipped.
highlights: Combine with Mehtab Bagh for a quieter day
kids_note: Wildlife + empty space = kid-friendly. Keep snacks hidden from langurs.

name: Mehtab Bagh
tagline: The 'Moonlight Garden' directly across the Yamuna from the Taj — best sunset view
why_visit: Built by Babur as the 11th of his Yamuna-front gardens; the Mehtab Bagh lines up perfectly axially with the Taj. Designed as moonlight-reflection garden. Today it's the premier sunset viewpoint of the Taj — better than Taj-side itself at that hour. Open until sunset.
highlights: Sunset view of the Taj from across the river
kids_note: Open grounds, stroller-friendly. Sunset is the experience — 5+ engage.

name: Itimad-ud-Daulah (Baby Taj)
tagline: Nur Jahan's 1628 tomb for her father — the design prototype for the Taj Mahal
why_visit: Built 1622-1628 by Empress Nur Jahan for her Persian father Mirza Ghiyas Beg. First Mughal tomb in all-white marble with pietra dura inlay — directly inspired the Taj Mahal two decades later. Much smaller scale, often near-empty. Across the Yamuna from central Agra, 8km.
kids_note: Small enough not to tire kids. Marble surfaces slippery when wet.

name: Tungabhadra Dam
tagline: 1953 dam across the Tungabhadra — gardens + musical fountain, 8km from Hampi
why_visit: Built post-independence (1945-1953) on the Tungabhadra river. Wide reservoir, gardens on the downstream side. Musical fountain show (7 PM daily), Japanese garden layout. Aquarium + boating. Crowded weekends, quiet weekdays.
kids_note: Family-friendly; fountain show is a hit for 4+.

name: Anegundi
tagline: The mythical Kishkindha — the older, quieter side of Hampi across the Tungabhadra
why_visit: Pre-Vijayanagara capital (14th-c), older than Hampi's main ruins. Believed in the Ramayana to be the monkey kingdom Kishkindha. Reach via coracle ride across the river (₹50-100) or the Virupapur Gaddi bridge. Ranganatha Temple, Pampa Sarovar, Anjaneya Hill (Hanuman's birthplace, 575 steps).
kids_note: 575-step climb is serious — skip for kids under 10. Coracle ride is kid-joy.

name: Matanga Hill
tagline: The highest point in Hampi — sunrise over Virupaksha + boulder-strewn landscape
why_visit: Sage Matanga's hill. ~30-min steep climb from Virupaksha Temple area. Top has Veerabhadra Temple (small). Sunrise view over the Virupaksha temple, Tungabhadra river, and the boulder-lunar landscape. Strenuous in the day's heat — pre-dawn climb is the move.
kids_note: Steep climb + loose rocks — not safe for kids under 12.

name: Sanapur Lake
tagline: Quiet lake on the Hippie Island side — coracle rides + sunset swims
why_visit: Artificial reservoir 2km from Virupapur Gaddi (Hippie Island). Coracle rides ₹100-200 per person. Swimming possible (freshwater, no crocs reported recently, but check with locals). Sunset spot for the post-Matanga crowd. Quieter than riverside.
kids_note: Natural lake, no lifeguards — not for kids under 12 swimming.
